draft.introduction: The Satyricon of Petronius (d. 66 CE) is the earliest surviving Roman novel. The banquet of Trimalchio, its most famous episode, satirizes the vulgarity of the newly rich and offers a darkly comic meditation on mortality and the futility of wealth.

translation.text: Alas for us wretches! How the whole of poor little man is nothing! So shall we all be, after the world below carries us off. Let us therefore live well while we are permitted to do so. Trimalchio brought out a silver skeleton, jointed so that its limbs could be twisted in every direction, and flung it upon the table, crying: Alas, alas, how wretched we are!
translation.original_text: Eheu nos miseros, quam totus homuncio nil est! Sic erimus cuncti, postquam nos auferet Orcus. Ergo vivamus, dum licet esse bene.

translator_notes.note: The silver skeleton at the banquet is a Roman version of the Egyptian custom of displaying a coffin at feasts. The memento mori theme is given a characteristically Petronian twist: Trimalchio’s vulgarity transforms philosophical reflection on death into grotesque entertainment.
